questions.q: Which instruments are powered by the pitot-static system?
choices: Attitude indicator, heading indicator, turn coordinator
Airspeed indicator, altimeter, vertical speed indicator
Only the altimeter
All six primary flight instruments
questions.explanation: Pitot-static system powers: Airspeed Indicator (pitot plus static pressure), Altimeter (static only), and Vertical Speed Indicator (static only). Gyroscopic instruments use vacuum or electrical power.

questions.q: Color of 100LL aviation gasoline?
choices: Red
Green
Blue
Colorless or straw-colored
questions.explanation: 100LL (low-lead) avgas is dyed blue. Regular 100 avgas is green. Jet-A turbine fuel is colorless or light straw-colored. Never use Jet-A in a piston engine.

questions.q: When is carburetor ice most likely to form?
choices: Only at temperatures below minus 20 C
At temperatures between 20 and 70 degrees F with high relative humidity even on clear days
Only when flying through rain or visible moisture
Only at night when temperatures drop rapidly
questions.explanation: Carburetor ice can form between 20 and 70 degrees F with high relative humidity. The venturi effect and fuel vaporization in the carburetor can lower its temperature up to 70 degrees F.

questions.q: A negative ammeter reading indicates?
choices: Battery is fully charged
Normal alternator charging operation
Alternator has failed and the battery is discharging to power the electrical system
Excessive engine RPM causing overcharge
questions.explanation: A negative ammeter (discharge) reading indicates the alternator is not producing sufficient power and the battery is supplying current to the electrical system. Shed electrical load and land.

questions.q: When should carburetor heat be applied?
choices: Only when engine roughness is already detected
Preventively any time conditions favor icing: 20 to 70 degrees F with visible moisture, fog, or high humidity
Only during descent and not during cruise
Never on a fuel-injected engine regardless of conditions
questions.explanation: Apply carb heat preventively when icing conditions exist. Apply full carb heat — partial carb heat can partially melt ice, allowing it to refreeze in a more dangerous location.

questions.q: Pitot tube fully blocked with no drain hole open — how does the ASI behave?
choices: ASI reads zero
ASI acts like an altimeter — reads higher when climbing and lower when descending
ASI freezes at the speed when blockage occurred
ASI is unaffected because the static port still functions
questions.explanation: With pitot fully blocked and no drain, trapped pressure cannot equalize with altitude changes. Climbing = ASI reads higher (acts like altimeter). Descending = reads lower.

questions.q: Purpose of the magneto check during runup?
choices: Ensure the battery is fully charged for start
Verify each magneto operates independently and that RPM drop is within published limits
Check alternator output and charging rate
Prime the engine fuel system for takeoff
questions.explanation: The magneto check verifies each magneto is operational by switching from BOTH to each individual magneto. Typical acceptable drop is 125 RPM maximum per magneto and 50 RPM maximum differential between the two.

questions.q: Alternate static source — effect on pitot-static instruments?
choices: No change — alternate static is perfectly calibrated
ASI reads lower, altimeter reads lower, VSI spikes upward
ASI slightly HIGH, altimeter slightly HIGH, VSI momentarily DOWN then corrects
Only the altimeter is affected; ASI and VSI unchanged
questions.explanation: Cockpit air is at slightly lower pressure than outside due to ram-air effects. Using alternate static: ASI reads slightly high, altimeter reads slightly high, and VSI deflects downward momentarily before correcting.

questions.q: What causes the graveyard spiral?
choices: A failed vacuum system causing the attitude indicator to tumble
The leans illusion causes the pilot to believe the banked turn is straight and level; pulling back to stop descent only tightens the spiral
Loss of engine power combined with a coordinated turn
Relying only on outside visual references in deteriorating visibility
questions.explanation: Graveyard spiral: the leans cause the pilot to sense a banked turn as straight and level. Pulling back to stop descent tightens the spiral rather than stopping it. Trust the attitude indicator.

questions.q: Water found in a fuel sump sample — what does it look like and what should you do?
choices: Blue tint throughout — normal fuel additive, safe to fly
Clear globules or drops at the bottom — drain multiple samples until obtaining pure blue fuel; do not fly with contaminated fuel
Cloudy appearance throughout — safe after agitating the fuel
Air bubbles at the surface — safe to fly
questions.explanation: Water is denser than avgas and sinks. It appears as clear globules or drops at the bottom of the sample cup. Drain multiple sump samples until obtaining pure blue fuel with no globules.

questions.q: Heading indicator precession — typical rate and corrective action?
choices: Up to 1 degree per minute — realign every 5 minutes
Up to 3 degrees per 15 minutes — realign with magnetic compass during straight-and-level unaccelerated flight every 10 to 15 minutes
Never precesses if the instrument is functioning properly
Fixed by the deviation card installed in the cockpit
questions.explanation: Gyroscopic precession causes the DI to drift up to 3 degrees per 15 minutes. Periodically realign with the magnetic compass during straight-and-level, unaccelerated, unturning flight.

questions.q: What is P-factor and when is it most pronounced?
choices: Power factor — ratio of engine efficiency to fuel consumption

Asymmetric blade effect: at high AoA the descending propeller blade has a greater angle of attack and produces more thrust than the ascending blade causing a left-turning tendency; most pronounced at high power and low airspeed
Precession of the directional gyro caused by aircraft maneuvering
Pitch factor relating angle of attack to lift coefficient in high-performance aircraft
questions.explanation: P-factor: at high angles of attack the descending (right-side) prop blade has a greater relative angle of attack and produces more thrust. This asymmetric thrust causes a left-turning tendency most pronounced in slow flight and climb.

questions.q: Fuel-injected engine vs carbureted engine — difference in icing risk?
choices: Fuel-injected engines have more carburetor ice risk
Fuel-injected engines cannot develop carburetor ice (no carburetor) but air induction icing can occur at the inlet or air filter
Fuel injection completely eliminates all icing-related risk
Both types of engines have identical icing risk under the same conditions
questions.explanation: No carburetor = no carburetor ice. However fuel-injected engines can suffer induction icing at the air filter or inlet in freezing fog or visible moisture conditions. An alternate air source is provided for this.

questions.q: Suction gauge reads below the green arc — what does this indicate?
choices: Normal operation — green arc is just a reference
Low engine oil pressure — unrelated to suction gauge
Vacuum system failure — attitude indicator and heading indicator may be unreliable; transition to partial panel procedures
Alternator failure — electrical instruments will fail next
questions.explanation: Suction gauge below the green arc (typically 4.5 to 5.4 in Hg) indicates insufficient vacuum for the vacuum-driven attitude indicator and heading indicator. These instruments may give unreliable or failing readings.

questions.q: What does gyroscopic rigidity mean and which instruments use this property?
choices: A spinning gyroscope resists changes to its axis of rotation — used in the attitude indicator and heading indicator
Gyroscopes always point toward magnetic north and are used in the magnetic compass
Gyroscopes spin in sync with engine RPM and are used in the tachometer
Gyroscopes resist vibration and are used in the altimeter for smoothing
questions.explanation: Rigidity in space: a spinning gyroscope resists any change to its axis orientation. Both the attitude indicator and heading indicator use this property. The turn coordinator uses gyroscopic precession rather than rigidity.

questions.q: What does the Vertical Speed Indicator (VSI) measure and does it have any lag?
choices: Actual instantaneous climb rate with no lag — most accurate instrument
Rate of change of static pressure indicating climb or descent rate — has a 6 to 9 second lag for accurate readings
Rate of change of airspeed used for energy management
Angle of attack converted to feet per minute — no lag
questions.explanation: The VSI uses a calibrated leak to measure the rate of change of static pressure. It shows trend immediately but requires 6 to 9 seconds to stabilize at the accurate rate.

questions.q: What does it mean when oil pressure reads zero immediately after engine start?
choices: Normal — takes time for oil to circulate
Indication of a serious lubrication failure — shut down immediately to prevent engine damage
Only a problem if the oil temperature also rises
The gauge is probably faulty — continue the flight
questions.explanation: Positive oil pressure should appear within approximately 30 seconds of engine start. If oil pressure does not register within this time, shut the engine down immediately. Operating without oil pressure causes catastrophic engine damage in seconds.

questions.q: Magnetic compass acceleration and turning errors — what do ANDS and OSUN mean?
choices: ANDS = Accurate North Detection System; OSUN = Old Sun navigation
Accelerate-North Decelerate-South on east-west headings. Overshoot-North Undershoot-South when turning through north and south in the northern hemisphere
ANDS and OSUN are not real aviation acronyms
They describe compass deviation in different geographic regions
questions.explanation: ANDS: On east or west headings, acceleration causes the compass to deflect toward North; deceleration toward South. OSUN: In turns the compass overshoots when turning to a northerly heading and undershoots turning to a southerly heading in the northern hemisphere.

questions.q: What is the function of the attitude indicator and what type of error can affect it?
choices: Shows magnetic heading — affected by compass variation
Shows pitch and bank attitude using gyroscopic rigidity — may show slight precession error after prolonged coordinated turns
Shows rate and direction of turn — affected by ball displacement
Shows vertical speed — affected by static port blockage
questions.explanation: The attitude indicator uses gyroscopic rigidity in space to display pitch and bank attitude relative to the horizon. After sustained coordinated turns, slight precession can occur causing a small apparent bank upon returning to straight and level.

questions.q: Oil temperature gauge reads in the red arc. What should you do?
choices: Continue the flight — red arcs are just advisory
Land as soon as practicable; reduce power, enrich mixture, check oil pressure, consider opening cowl flaps to increase cooling
Increase power to generate more oil circulation
Ignore it if the oil pressure is still in the green
questions.explanation: Red arc on any engine instrument indicates a dangerous operating condition. High oil temperature can indicate low oil quantity, a blocked oil cooler, or excessive engine load. Reduce power, allow cooling, and land as soon as practicable.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the primer before engine start?
choices: To lubricate the cylinders before startup
To inject fuel directly into the cylinders or intake manifold to aid cold engine starting by enriching the initial mixture
To pressurize the fuel system for the fuel injectors
To purge air from the fuel lines before start
questions.explanation: The primer injects raw fuel directly into the cylinders or intake manifold to create a combustible mixture for cold starts. Excess priming can cause hydraulic lock or fire. Always follow POH instructions.

questions.q: What is torque as a left-turning tendency and when is it most pronounced?
choices: Engine vibration — affects all flight phases equally
Reaction to the clockwise engine and propeller rotation causes the aircraft to roll to the left; most pronounced at high power and low airspeed
Torque only affects tailwheel aircraft
Torque is only present during aerobatic maneuvers
questions.explanation: Torque: Newton's third law — clockwise engine/propeller rotation causes an equal and opposite counterclockwise (left roll) reaction on the aircraft. Most pronounced at high power and low airspeed such as during takeoff and climb.

questions.q: What does a positive ammeter reading indicate?
choices: Battery is discharging and needs replacement
The alternator is producing power and the electrical system is operating normally
Excessive electrical load — shed unnecessary equipment
Alternator failure with battery backup active
questions.explanation: A positive ammeter reading indicates the alternator is producing power and the electrical system is operating normally. Monitor for any sudden change to a negative reading.

questions.q: The turn coordinator displays what information?
choices: Only the rate of turn in degrees per second
Rate of turn and coordination (slip or skid) via the ball — standard rate equals 3 degrees per second or 360 degrees in 2 minutes
Only slip or skid via the inclinometer ball
Pitch attitude and bank angle like the attitude indicator
questions.explanation: Turn coordinator displays: (1) rate and approximate direction of turn via the miniature aircraft symbol, and (2) coordination via the inclinometer ball. Standard rate turn is 3 degrees per second (360 in 2 minutes).

questions.q: Pitot tube blocked with drain hole open — how does the ASI behave?
choices: ASI acts like an altimeter
ASI reads zero because no pitot pressure reaches the instrument
ASI freezes at the speed when blockage occurred
ASI reads correctly from static port alone
questions.explanation: When the pitot tube is blocked but the drain hole is open, pitot pressure drains away. The ASI reads zero because there is no pitot pressure to create a differential with static. Unlike fully blocked (no drain) which acts like an altimeter.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the aircraft's fuel selector valve and what are the common positions?
choices: The fuel selector valve controls fuel flow rate to the engine by adjusting a metering orifice. Positions include HIGH FLOW, LOW FLOW, and OFF — pilots select HIGH FLOW for takeoff and LOW FLOW for cruise to improve fuel economy.

The fuel selector valve controls which fuel tank(s) feed the engine. Common positions: LEFT (left tank only), RIGHT (right tank only), BOTH (both tanks simultaneously), OFF. Some aircraft require specific takeoff and landing positions per the POH.

The fuel selector valve routes fuel either through the gascolator for filtration or directly to the carburetor for high-demand operations. Positions include FILTERED, DIRECT, and OFF, and must be set to FILTERED before starting.

The fuel selector valve isolates the fuel system from the vent lines during pressurization. Positions are PRESSURIZED, VENTED, and OFF — always set to VENTED before landing to prevent vapor lock in the fuel lines.
questions.explanation: The fuel selector valve directs fuel from the selected tank(s) to the engine. Improper fuel management (running a tank dry, improper position for takeoff/landing) is a leading cause of engine failures. Always follow POH procedures for fuel selector position during each phase of flight.

questions.q: What is the function of the aircraft's vacuum system and what instruments depend on it?
choices: The electrical system uses alternator-driven AC inverters to power the gyroscopic attitude indicator and heading indicator through dedicated bus bars. Most light aircraft have a single alternator — failure leaves these instruments unreliable.

The vacuum system uses engine-driven vacuum pumps to drive the gyroscopic attitude indicator and heading indicator through suction cups. Most light aircraft have a single vacuum pump — failure leaves these instruments unreliable.

The hydraulic system uses engine-driven hydraulic pumps to drive the gyroscopic attitude indicator and heading indicator through fluid pressure lines. Most light aircraft have a single hydraulic pump — failure leaves these instruments unreliable.

The pitot-static system uses ram air pressure ports to drive the gyroscopic attitude indicator and heading indicator through sealed instrument cases. Most light aircraft have a single pitot probe — blockage leaves these instruments unreliable.
questions.explanation: The vacuum (suction) system uses an engine-driven pump to spin the gyroscopes in the attitude indicator and heading indicator. A vacuum failure makes these instruments unreliable within minutes. The turn coordinator is typically electrically powered and remains operational after vacuum failure. Check suction gauge in preflight.

questions.q: What is a magneto and why do aircraft typically have two?
choices: A magneto is a battery-driven high-voltage coil that boosts starter current to the spark plugs during engine start — independent of the alternator. Two magnetos ensure reliable starting even when one coil is weak or fails.

A magneto is a self-contained high-voltage electrical generator that provides ignition to the engine spark plugs — independent of the aircraft's electrical system. Two magnetos provide engine redundancy so the engine continues operating if one magneto fails.

A magneto is a permanent-magnet alternator that charges the aircraft battery during flight — independent of the engine-driven alternator. Two magnetos provide redundant battery charging so avionics stay powered if one magneto fails.

A magneto is a voltage-regulator component that stabilizes generator output to the ignition system — independent of the bus voltage. Two magnetos are used to balance the electrical load equally across both sides of the ignition harness.
questions.explanation: Magnetos are self-contained electrical generators that produce high-voltage current for spark plugs independent of the aircraft's battery and alternator. Having two magnetos (each firing one plug per cylinder) provides redundancy — if one magneto fails, the engine continues on the other. This is checked during the runup.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the landing gear extension and retraction system position indicator?
choices: The landing gear position indicator shows hydraulic system pressure in each gear actuator — typically with gauges: green for normal pressure, amber for low pressure, and red for critically low pressure requiring an alternate extension.

The landing gear position indicator shows whether the gear is up-and-locked, down-and-locked, or in transit — typically with lights: green for down-and-locked, red for in-transit or unsafe, and no light for gear up and locked on some aircraft

The landing gear position indicator shows the remaining brake pad thickness on each wheel assembly — typically with lights: green for adequate wear, amber for inspect-soon, and red for replace-before-next-flight on each gear leg.

The landing gear position indicator shows nose-wheel steering alignment relative to the runway centerline — typically with lights: green for aligned, amber for slight deflection, and red for a steering-limit exceedance on the ground.
questions.explanation: The landing gear position indicator typically uses lights (green = down and locked, red/amber = in transit or unsafe, dark = up and locked on some aircraft) to indicate landing gear position. Some aircraft use mechanically displayed indicators. Always verify gear position using both the indicator and any available audible warnings.

questions.q: What is a fuel-injected engine's throttle body and how does it differ from a carburetor?
choices: A fuel injection system uses a carburetor mounted downstream of the throttle plate that atomizes fuel using a venturi and delivers it to a common plenum chamber. The throttle body in fuel-injected engines simply replaces the carburetor float bowl with an electronically metered needle valve, reducing icing risk at the venturi throat.

A fuel injection system meters fuel directly into each cylinder's intake port (port injection) or into the intake manifold (throttle body injection) rather than mixing it in a venturi as in a carburetor. Benefits include more precise fuel metering, no carburetor ice risk, and better fuel distribution.

A throttle body in a fuel-injected engine serves the same function as the accelerator pump in a carburetor — providing an enriched fuel charge during rapid throttle advancement to prevent lean stumble. Unlike carburetors, fuel injection systems meter fuel by monitoring manifold pressure and engine RPM via analog pressure diaphragms.

A fuel-injected engine's throttle body regulates airflow via a butterfly valve identical to a carburetor's venturi, but draws fuel from a high-pressure injection pump rather than a float bowl. The primary advantage over carburetion is that the injector nozzles are self-draining, preventing fuel pooling and hot-start vapor lock issues.
questions.explanation: Fuel injection systems deliver fuel directly to each cylinder intake port or throttle body, bypassing the carburetor venturi. This eliminates carburetor icing, provides more precise fuel metering, better fuel distribution among cylinders, and generally better performance. However, fuel-injected engines can experience hot-start difficulties and vapor lock.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the primer in a fuel-injected engine vs a carbureted engine?
choices: Both carbureted and fuel-injected engines use a mechanically identical primer that injects fuel into the intake manifold before cranking. The only difference is pressure — carbureted primers operate at 2-4 PSI while fuel-injected primers use the system's full rail pressure of 14-45 PSI.

Carbureted engines use a primer to inject fuel into cylinder(s) for cold starts. Many fuel-injected engines do not have traditional primers but instead use the throttle position and boost pump to prime the system — specific cold start procedures vary significantly by aircraft.

Carbureted engines do not use primers — the accelerator pump provides adequate fuel enrichment during cold cranking. Primers are used exclusively in fuel-injected engines to purge vapor from injection lines before start; failure to prime for at least 5 seconds risks a lean misfire during cranking.

Primers in carbureted engines inject fuel downstream of the throttle plate, bypassing the venturi entirely. Fuel-injected engines need no primer because continuous-flow fuel injection maintains positive pressure at each cylinder nozzle at all times — including when the engine is stopped and cold.
questions.explanation: In carbureted engines, the primer injects fuel directly into cylinders for cold starts. Many fuel-injected engines use a different cold-start procedure involving the throttle and electric fuel boost pump to prime the fuel injection system — the POH cold-start procedure must be followed precisely to avoid flooding or fire.

questions.q: What are the four forces acting on an aircraft in flight and when are they balanced?
choices: Lift, drag, weight, and thrust — balanced during climbing only
Lift, drag, weight, and thrust act on an aircraft at all times. They are in equilibrium (balanced) during straight-and-level, unaccelerated flight when lift equals weight and thrust equals drag.
Lift and weight are always equal; thrust and drag are only equal at cruise
Only two forces act on an aircraft — gravity and engine thrust
questions.explanation: The four forces are: Lift (opposes weight, acts perpendicular to flightpath), Weight (gravity, acts toward Earth's center), Thrust (acts along flightpath, forward), Drag (opposes motion, acts along flightpath, rearward). In straight-and-level, unaccelerated flight: Lift = Weight and Thrust = Drag.

questions.q: What is Vne and what does exceeding it risk?
choices: Vne (Never Exceed Speed) is the maximum speed allowed during normal cruise operations; exceeding it briefly in smooth air is permitted up to 110% Vne before the manufacturer requires an airframe inspection per the maintenance manual.

Vne (Never Exceed Speed) is the maximum speed for the aircraft in any flight operation. Exceeding Vne risks structural failure or control surface flutter, which can cause catastrophic airframe failure. Shown as a red line on the airspeed indicator.

Vne (Never Exceed Speed) is the maximum speed above which flap operation is prohibited; exceeding it with flaps extended risks flap structural failure but poses no danger to the basic airframe or primary control surfaces.

Vne (Never Exceed Speed) is the maximum descent speed permitted during emergency procedures; exceeding it in a spiral dive risks exceeding limit load on the empennage but the wings are structurally protected by a separate Vno limit.
questions.explanation: Vne (Never Exceed Speed) is the speed above which structural damage or failure may occur due to aeroelastic flutter, control surface flutter, or exceeding design load limits. It is shown as a red radial line on the airspeed indicator. Never exceed Vne under any circumstances.

questions.q: What color coding is used on the airspeed indicator and what does each color represent?
choices: Airspeed indicators have no color coding — only numbers
White arc: flap operating range (Vs0 to Vfe). Green arc: normal operating range (Vs1 to Vno). Yellow arc: caution range — smooth air only (Vno to Vne). Red line: never exceed speed (Vne).
Green arc is the takeoff range; red line is cruise speed
White arc is IFR range; green arc is VFR range
questions.explanation: Airspeed indicator color coding: White arc (flap range) = Vs0 (stall, gear/flaps down) to Vfe (maximum flap extension). Green arc (normal ops) = Vs1 (stall, clean) to Vno (maximum structural cruise). Yellow arc (caution — smooth air only) = Vno to Vne. Red radial line = Vne (never exceed).

questions.q: What is gyroscopic precession and which instrument uses this property?
choices: Gyroscopic rigidity: a force applied to a spinning gyroscope causes it to react 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation. The attitude indicator uses rigidity — a bank input (ailerons) causes the gyro to maintain orientation and display a pitch-attitude reading.

Gyroscopic precession: a force applied to a spinning gyroscope causes it to react 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation. The turn coordinator uses precession — a yaw input (rudder) causes the gyro to precess and display a bank-rate indication.

Gyroscopic precession: a force applied to a spinning gyroscope causes it to react 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation. The heading indicator uses precession — a pitch input (elevator) causes the gyro to precess and display a heading-change indication.

Gyroscopic tumbling: a force applied to a spinning gyroscope causes it to react 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation. The attitude indicator uses tumbling — a bank input (ailerons) causes the gyro to tumble and display a roll-rate indication.
questions.explanation: Gyroscopic precession: when a force is applied to a spinning gyroscope, the effect appears 90 degrees later in the direction of rotation. The turn coordinator (TC) uses precession — a yaw input causes the gimbal-mounted gyro to precess, deflecting the miniature aircraft to indicate rate of turn. The TC is NOT a direct bank angle indicator.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the aircraft's position lights (navigation lights) and when are they required?
choices: Required only during IFR flight
Position lights show aircraft position and direction to other pilots at night — red (left wing), green (right wing), white (tail); required from sunset to sunrise
Required at all times day and night
Required only near airports
questions.explanation: Position lights (navigation lights) are required from sunset to sunrise and during IMC conditions per 14 CFR 91.209. Colors: red (left/port), green (right/starboard), white (aft). Other pilots use them to determine aircraft heading for collision avoidance.

questions.q: What does the symbol at the top of the yellow arc on the airspeed indicator represent?
choices: Vfe — maximum flap extension speed
Vne — never-exceed speed; the red radial line is the absolute maximum — exceeding it risks structural failure
Va — maneuvering speed
Vy — best rate of climb
questions.explanation: Vne (never-exceed speed) is marked by the red radial line at the high end of the yellow caution arc. The yellow arc itself is the caution range — smooth air only. Exceeding Vne risks structural failure. It is the absolute maximum operating speed.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the vacuum system and which instruments does it power?
choices: Creates pressure to drive gyroscopes in the turn coordinator and magnetic compass; a venturi tube driven by ram airflow generates suction; the pressure gauge monitors system health (normal 4.5-5.4 in Hg)

Creates suction to spin gyroscopes in the attitude indicator and heading indicator; a vacuum pump driven by the engine generates suction; the suction gauge monitors system health (normal 4.5-5.4 in Hg)

Creates suction to spin gyroscopes in the turn coordinator and airspeed indicator; an electric vacuum pump driven by the battery generates suction; the suction gauge monitors system health (normal 2.0-3.5 in Hg)

Creates pressure to operate gyroscopes in the attitude indicator and altimeter; a vacuum pump driven by the engine generates pressure; the manifold pressure gauge monitors system health (normal 4.5-5.4 in Hg)
questions.explanation: The vacuum (suction) system powers gyroscopic instruments — typically the attitude indicator and heading indicator. An engine-driven pump creates suction that spins the gyro rotors. Loss of vacuum causes these instruments to fail — transition to partial panel. Monitor the suction gauge.

questions.q: What is the arm in a weight and balance calculation?
choices: The total weight of all items combined
The horizontal distance in inches from the datum to the item's center of gravity
The vertical distance from the aircraft's center to the ground
The maximum allowable weight at a given CG position
questions.explanation: The arm is the horizontal distance in inches from the datum (a reference point defined in the aircraft's type certificate) to the center of gravity of each item. Positive values are aft of the datum.

questions.q: How is moment calculated in weight and balance?
choices: Moment = Total Weight / CG location
Moment = Weight x Arm
Moment = Arm / Weight
Moment = Total Weight - Empty Weight
questions.explanation: Moment = Weight x Arm. It represents the turning force about the datum. Sum all moments and divide by total weight to find the overall CG location.

questions.q: What is the most dangerous condition regarding center of gravity?
choices: CG too far forward — requires more elevator back pressure
CG too far aft — reduces longitudinal stability, may make stall recovery impossible
CG at the forward limit — aircraft is underpowered
CG at the aft limit — aircraft stalls at lower airspeeds
questions.explanation: An aft CG is the most dangerous condition. The aircraft becomes longitudinally unstable and extremely pitch-sensitive. A stall may be unrecoverable. An aft CG also reduces the effective angle of attack range.

questions.q: Avgas 100LL weighs approximately how many pounds per gallon?
choices: 5 lbs/gal
6 lbs/gal
7 lbs/gal
8 lbs/gal
questions.explanation: Aviation gasoline (100LL) weighs approximately 6 pounds per gallon. This is used in weight and balance calculations. Jet-A weighs approximately 6.7-6.8 lbs/gal.

questions.q: During flight, fuel burns off from the tanks. How does this typically affect CG?
choices: CG never changes during flight
CG moves depending on the location of the fuel tanks relative to the overall CG — must verify CG stays within limits throughout flight
CG always moves forward as fuel burns
CG always moves aft as fuel burns
questions.explanation: As fuel burns, the CG shifts in the direction of the fuel tanks relative to the loaded CG. If fuel tanks are aft of the CG, burning fuel moves CG forward and vice versa. Always verify the CG remains within limits from takeoff weight to landing weight.

questions.q: What is the useful load of an aircraft?
choices: The weight of the aircraft with full fuel and no passengers
Maximum Gross Weight minus Basic Empty Weight
The total payload capacity including fuel but not crew
The maximum allowable cargo weight only
questions.explanation: Useful load = Maximum Gross Weight minus Basic Empty Weight. It represents the total weight available for fuel, passengers, cargo, and other items. Payload = useful load minus fuel weight.

questions.q: What is adverse yaw and how is it corrected?
choices: A yaw caused by crosswind — corrected by crabbing into the wind

When ailerons are applied, the descending wing's aileron creates more drag, causing the nose to yaw opposite to the intended turn. Corrected with coordinated rudder input in the direction of the turn.
A yaw caused by propeller torque during high-power operations
Adverse yaw only occurs during stall entry and recovery
questions.explanation: Adverse yaw: the downward-deflecting aileron (on the rising wing) creates induced drag, while the upward-deflecting aileron (on the descending wing) also creates drag. The net effect is a yaw opposite to the intended turn. Corrected with rudder — the ball provides real-time feedback on coordination.

questions.q: A constant-speed propeller aircraft has both a throttle and a propeller control. When increasing power for takeoff, what is the correct sequence?
choices: Always advance the prop control first, then the throttle
Advance the throttle first to desired manifold pressure, then adjust the prop control to desired RPM
Simultaneously advance both controls equally
The sequence does not matter — both controls do the same thing
questions.explanation: For constant-speed prop aircraft: when INCREASING power, advance the prop control first (increase RPM), then advance the throttle (increase manifold pressure). This prevents the engine from over-boosting. When DECREASING power: throttle first (reduce MP), then prop (reduce RPM). Remember: power up = prop first; power down = throttle first.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the alternate air (alternate air source) on a fuel-injected engine?
choices: It provides an emergency fuel source if the main fuel pump fails
It bypasses the air filter and draws warm air from inside the cowling to prevent induction icing if the primary inlet becomes blocked
It provides extra cooling air to prevent engine overheating
It is the same as carburetor heat on a carbureted engine
questions.explanation: Fuel-injected engines cannot develop carburetor ice (no carburetor) but can develop induction system icing at the air filter or inlet. The alternate air door bypasses the normal filtered air intake and draws warm, unfiltered air from inside the cowling. Activate if you experience a sudden unexplained power loss in icing conditions.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes a 406 MHz ELT compared to an older 121.5 MHz ELT?
choices: 406 MHz ELTs are no longer required — 121.5 MHz is the current standard

406 MHz ELTs transmit digitally with aircraft registration and GPS position (if equipped), are monitored by COSPAS-SARSAT satellites, and enable much faster SAR response than the older 121.5 MHz analog units
Both types are monitored by satellites with equal effectiveness
121.5 MHz ELTs have longer battery life and are preferred for most aircraft
questions.explanation: 406 MHz ELTs: digital encoding with aircraft ID and GPS position (if GPS equipped), monitored by international COSPAS-SARSAT satellite system, typical location accuracy of 100 meters with GPS. 121.5 MHz ELTs have NOT been monitored by satellites since February 1, 2009. 406 MHz is now required on most aircraft and is far superior for SAR purposes.
